# Results

## Data trimming

The data set for accuracy included 35 participants contained a total of 6720 data points (192 trials x 35 participants) and maintained all correct and incorrect trials. 
Due to an error, three stimulus items were duplicated in the data (two non-cognates and one double cognate) and were subsequently omitted from further analysis, resulting in a total of 6510 data points (186 per participant).
For reaction time the analyzed data frame contained 2180 data points after removing pseudoword trials (n = `r ps_removed`), incorrect trials (n = `r incorrect`), trials that were too fast (n = `r too_fast`) or too slow (n = `r too_slow`). Some trials fit multiple criteria for removal, and a trial was considered too fast or too slow when the reaction time was under 500ms or over 2 seconds.

## Accuracy


```{r accuracy, fig.cap="Percentage of correct responses by word type"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("docs", "plots", "overall_acc.png"))
```

Figure \@ref(fig:accuracy) shows the overall accuracy within the entire data set (`r nrow(p_data)` trials) excluding pseudowords (in which `r round2(totals_n$pct_correct[2])`% of trials were correct).
Overall, three-way cognates were the most accurate (`r round2(totals_n$pct_correct[3])`%), followed by two-way cognates (`r round2(totals_n$pct_correct[4])`%), while non-cognates were the least accurate (`r round2(totals_n$pct_correct[1])`%).

Figure \@ref(fig:accuracyind) shows histograms of individuals' percentage of correct trials in each word-type. 
Individual accuracy in non-cognates ranged from `r round2(range(neat_ind_data$non_cognate)[1])` % of trials correct to `r round2(range(neat_ind_data$non_cognate)[2])` (median `r round2(median(neat_ind_data$non_cognate))`%)
Like the data set as a whole, the median of individual percentages correct for the three-way cognates was the highest `r round2(median(neat_ind_data$three_way_cognate))`% (range `r round2(range(neat_ind_data$three_way_cognate)[1])`% - `r round2(range(neat_ind_data$three_way_cognate)[2])`), followed by two-way cognates (median percentage correct = `r round2(median(neat_ind_data$two_way_cognate))`%; range = `r round2(range(neat_ind_data$two_way_cognate)[1])`% - `r round2(range(neat_ind_data$two_way_cognate)[2])`).
Taken together, these descriptive results suggest that, while most participants benefit from cognates in accuracy, a minority also experience steep drops in accuracy.
Indeed, two participants showed the opposite of expected and were most accurate in non-cognates (60.4% and 68.7%) and much less accurate in two-way (25% and 16.7%) and three-way cognates (20.8% and 8.3%).

```{r accuracyind, fig.cap="Histograms of individual percentages corrects by word type"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("docs", "plots", "ind_acc.png"))
```


```{r}
acc_table = describe_posterior(accuracy_mod_b) %>% 
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 2))) %>% 
  mutate(CI = paste0("[", CI_low, ", ", CI_high, "]")) %>% 
  mutate(ROPE = paste0("[", ROPE_low, ", ", ROPE_high, "]")) %>% 
  select(Parameter, Median, CI, pd, ROPE, ROPE_Percentage, Rhat, ESS)



acc_t <- flextable(acc_table, use_labels = FALSE) %>% 
  set_caption(caption = "Posterior Distribution of the Accuracy Model") %>% 
  font(fontname = "Times New Roman", part = "all") %>% 
  theme_booktabs() %>% 
  align(align = "center", part = "all") %>% 
  align(j = 1, align = "left", part = "body") %>% 
  set_header_labels(
  CI = "95% CI",
  ROPE_Percentage = "% in ROPE"
) %>% 
  autofit()
  
acc_t
```

Overall, there was strong evidence for a cognate effect in accuracy, and somewhat weaker evidence for a cumulative effect in accuracy.
Figure \@ref(fig:accmod) visualizes the posterior distribution for the accuracy model and Table 2 includes specific estimates of each model term. 
In this model, the intercept was `r acc_table$Median[1]` and represents the log-odds of an accurate response in the reference condition (two-way cognates) when the adapted LexTALE scores (proficiency_z) and lexical frequency (frequency_z) are average.
In practice, this corresponds to a participant with average proficiency would be accurate about `r round(plogis(acc_table$Median[1]), digits = 3)*100`% of the time for words of average lexical frequency. 
The effect for non-cognates was `r acc_table$Median[2]``r acc_table$CI[2]`. `r acc_table$pd[2]`, which corresponds to a lower predicted probability of a correct response (`r round(plogis(acc_table$Median[1] + acc_table$Median[2]), digits = 3)*100`%).
The probability of direction was 1.00, signaling that virtually the entire posterior distribution is below zero, providing extremely strong evidence for a negative effect.
Additionally, 0% of the posterior distribution fell within the ROPE, suggesting that none of the credible parameter values were practically equivalent to zero.
For three-way cognates, the median effect was `r acc_table$Median[3]` `r acc_table$CI[3]`, indicating that the most credible values of the effect were positive. 
The effect was associated with a pd of `r acc_table$pd[3]`, indicating that approximately 98% of the posterior distribution supported a positive effect. 
Additionally, only `r acc_table$ROPE_Percentage[3]*100`% of the posterior distribution fell within the ROPE, suggesting that most credible parameter values were not practically equivalent to zero. 
Taken together, these results provide evidence that three-way cognates were identified more accurately than two-way cognates, although the evidence was somewhat weaker than that observed for the non-cognate effect.

Higher numbers for both the continuous predictors lexical frequency and proficiency were associated with higher accuracy.
For lexical frequency, the median estimate was `r acc_table$Median[4]`  `r acc_table$CI[4]`, indicating that a one standard deviation increase in lexical frequency was associated with an increase of `r acc_table$Median[4]` increase in the log-odds of a correct response. 
Holding all other predictors constant, predicted accuracy increases to around `r round(plogis(acc_table$Median[1] + acc_table$Median[4]), digits = 3)*100`% words that were 1 standard deviation more frequent than average, while it dropped to `r round(plogis(acc_table$Median[1] - acc_table$Median[4]), digits = 3)*100`% for words that were one standard deviation less frequent than average. 
Again, the pd (`r acc_table$pd[4]`) and ROPE percentage `r acc_table$ROPE_Percentage[4]`% provide strong evidence for the existence of a positive association between lexical frequency and accuracy. 
Proficiency was also shown the be positively associated with accuracy; the estimate `r acc_table$Median[4]` `r acc_table$CI[4]`] suggests that 
For proficiency, the median estimate was `r acc_table$Median[5]` `r acc_table$CI[5]`, once again indicating that a one standard deviation increase in lexical frequency was associated with an increase of `r acc_table$Median[5]` increase in the log-odds of a correct response. 
Predicted accuracy increases to around `r round(plogis(acc_table$Median[1] + acc_table$Median[4]), digits = 3)*100`% by participants that were 1 standard deviation more proficient than average, while it dropped to `r round(plogis(acc_table$Median[1] - acc_table$Median[5]), digits = 3)*100`% for participants that were one standard deviation less proficient than average. 
Like frequency, the pd (`r acc_table$pd[5]`) and ROPE percentage (`r acc_table$ROPE_Percentage[5]`) provide compelling evidence of that the effect is positive and not negligible. 


```{r accmod, fig.cap="Posterior Distributions of the accuracy model"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("docs", "plots", "acc_mod.png"))

```

## Reaction time

Overall, there was evidence of a general cognate effect in reaction times, but not a cumulative one. 


```{r overt, fig.cap="Mean reaction time of each word type"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("docs", "plots", "over_rt.png"))

```


```{r indrt, fig.cap="Individual effects in reaction times"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("docs", "plots", "ind_rt.png"))

```


```{r retmod, fig.cap="Posterior Distributions of the reaction time model"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("docs", "plots", "rt_mod.png"))

```
